title: 05 - Diseases of the Immune System - Flashcards
description: Third-party generated Chapter 5 flashcards optimized for active recall, spaced repetition, and exam revision. The deck prioritizes high-yield mechanisms, disease prototypes, immune pathways, transplant pathology, immunodeficiency patterns, HIV/AIDS, and amyloidosis.

disclaimer.endorsement_notice: These flashcards are third-party generated study materials. They are not produced by, reviewed by, approved by, endorsed by, or affiliated with the textbook authors, Elsevier, the Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia, or any other authority, institution, publisher, or examining body.
disclaimer.use_note: Use these flashcards as revision support alongside the original source material.

source_notes.source_title: Robbins Basic Pathology, 10th Edition, Chapter 5: Diseases of the Immune System
RCPA - Basic Pathological Sciences Syllabus 2026 - October 2025
source_notes.organization: Royal College of Pathologists of Australasia
source_notes.reference_note: Used for topic emphasis, exam framing, and revision-bucket structure.

source_notes.source_title: Chapter 5 Study Notes and Exam Prep Questions created from the approved Chapter 5 source set
source_notes.reference_note: Used to compress Chapter 5 into single-fact active-recall prompts.

deck.deck_name: Pathology::Chapter 05 - Diseases of the Immune System
deck.design_note: Cards are kept mostly single-fact and under one question per card for rapid recall.
deck.page_reference_note: The source_page_refs arrays use the printed book page numbers from Robbins Basic Pathology 10th Edition, not the sequential PDF page position. Chapter 5 occupies book pages 121-188.

cards.front: What are the main components of innate immunity emphasized in Chapter 5?
cards.back: Epithelial barriers, phagocytes, dendritic cells, NK cells, and complement or other plasma proteins.

cards.front: Which cell is the most important antigen-presenting cell for initiating T-cell responses to protein antigens?
cards.back: The dendritic cell.

cards.front: What cytokine is generated when inflammasome-associated caspase-1 cleaves its precursor?
cards.back: IL-1.

cards.front: What does class I MHC mainly present, and to which T-cell subset?
cards.back: It presents cytosolic peptides to CD8+ T cells.

cards.front: What does class II MHC mainly present, and to which T-cell subset?
cards.back: It presents peptides from extracellular proteins to CD4+ T cells.

cards.front: What helper-T-cell interaction is required for immunoglobulin class switching?
cards.back: CD40L on the T cell binding CD40 on the B cell, together with cytokines.

cards.front: What is the key host-defense role of TH17 cells?
cards.back: Recruiting neutrophils and monocytes against some extracellular bacteria and fungi.

cards.front: Which cell is the key immediate effector in type I hypersensitivity after IgE cross-linking?
cards.back: The mast cell.

cards.front: Which cytokine is most directly responsible for class switching to IgE in immediate hypersensitivity?
cards.back: IL-4.

cards.front: Which inflammatory cell is especially important in the late-phase tissue injury of immediate hypersensitivity?
cards.back: The eosinophil.

cards.front: What is the core immunologic mechanism in type II hypersensitivity?
cards.back: IgG or IgM directed against fixed cell or tissue antigens, causing phagocytosis, complement-mediated inflammation, or receptor dysfunction.

cards.front: What is the classic systemic prototype of type III hypersensitivity?
cards.back: Serum sickness.

cards.front: When does the classic PPD delayed-type hypersensitivity reaction peak?
cards.back: At about 24 to 72 hours.

cards.front: What activated macrophage form is the core cellular component of a granuloma?
cards.back: The epithelioid macrophage, often accompanied by giant cells.

cards.front: What is the principal mechanism of central T-cell tolerance?
cards.back: Deletion of self-reactive immature T cells in the thymus.

cards.front: What is the main immune function of AIRE?
cards.back: It promotes thymic expression of peripheral self antigens so self-reactive T cells can be deleted.

cards.front: In central tolerance, what can some self-reactive immature B cells do instead of undergoing deletion?
cards.back: They can undergo receptor editing and generate a new, non-self-reactive antigen receptor.

cards.front: Which transcription factor is the signature marker of the best-defined regulatory T cells?
cards.back: FOXP3.

cards.front: Which two cytokines are strongly associated with the suppressive function of regulatory T cells?
cards.back: IL-10 and TGF-beta.

cards.front: What does molecular mimicry mean in autoimmunity?
cards.back: A microbial antigen resembles a self antigen closely enough that the immune response cross-reacts with host tissue.

cards.front: What is epitope spreading?
cards.back: Expansion of an autoimmune response to self epitopes that were not the original targets, often after tissue injury exposes new antigens.

cards.front: Which autoantibody is highly specific for systemic lupus erythematosus?
cards.back: Anti-Sm antibody.

cards.front: Which lupus autoantibody is strongly associated with nephritis?
cards.back: Anti-double-stranded DNA antibody.

cards.front: What is the coagulation paradox of anti-phospholipid antibodies in lupus?
cards.back: They can prolong clotting tests in vitro but cause thrombosis and pregnancy loss in vivo.

cards.front: In diffuse lupus nephritis, what do wire-loop lesions represent?
cards.back: Extensive subendothelial immune-complex deposits that thicken glomerular capillary walls.

cards.front: Which autoantibodies are classically associated with Sjogren syndrome?
cards.back: Anti-Ro / SS-A and anti-La / SS-B.

cards.front: What major malignancy risk is increased in Sjogren syndrome?
cards.back: B-cell lymphoma, especially arising in salivary glands or other extranodal sites.

cards.front: What three processes define the pathogenesis of systemic sclerosis?
cards.back: Autoimmunity, obliterative microvascular disease, and excessive fibrosis.

cards.front: Which autoantibody is associated with limited systemic sclerosis or the CREST pattern?
cards.back: Anti-centromere antibody.

cards.front: Which autoantibody is associated with diffuse systemic sclerosis and a greater likelihood of pulmonary fibrosis?
cards.back: Anti-topoisomerase I, also called anti-Scl-70.

cards.front: What are the major antigenic differences between donor and recipient that drive graft rejection?
cards.back: Differences in HLA alleles.

cards.front: What causes hyperacute graft rejection?
cards.back: Preformed recipient antibodies binding graft endothelial antigens, with complement activation and thrombosis.

cards.front: What biopsy finding is a classic clue to acute cellular rejection in a kidney graft?
cards.back: Tubulitis, often with interstitial inflammation and endotheliitis.

cards.front: What pathologic marker strongly supports acute antibody-mediated rejection in a kidney allograft?
cards.back: Complement deposition, especially C4d, in peritubular capillaries with capillaritis or glomerulitis.

cards.front: What is the hallmark vascular lesion of chronic graft rejection?
cards.back: Graft arteriosclerosis with progressive intimal thickening and luminal narrowing.

cards.front: Which organs are classically targeted in acute graft-versus-host disease?
cards.back: Skin, liver, and gastrointestinal tract.

cards.front: What is the defining immune problem in severe combined immunodeficiency (SCID)?
cards.back: Failure of T-cell development with major defects in both cellular and humoral immunity.

cards.front: In X-linked SCID, which cytokine signal is most important for the lymphocyte-development defect?
cards.back: IL-7 signaling through the common gamma chain.

cards.front: Which kinase is defective in X-linked agammaglobulinemia?
cards.back: Bruton tyrosine kinase (BTK).

cards.front: What developmental defect underlies DiGeorge syndrome?
cards.back: Failure of thymic development from the third and fourth pharyngeal pouches.

cards.front: What molecular defect most commonly causes X-linked hyper-IgM syndrome?
cards.back: CD40L deficiency on helper T cells.

cards.front: What is the typical antibody-production problem in common variable immunodeficiency?
cards.back: Hypogammaglobulinemia, often with normal numbers of mature B cells but absent plasma cells.

cards.front: What infection pattern is classically associated with selective IgA deficiency?
cards.back: Recurrent mucosal infections, especially sinopulmonary infections and giardiasis.

cards.front: What enzyme defect causes chronic granulomatous disease?
cards.back: Defective phagocyte oxidase, causing failure of the oxidative burst.

cards.front: What two host-cell receptors are required for HIV entry into target cells?
cards.back: CD4 plus a chemokine coreceptor, usually CCR5 or CXCR4.

cards.front: What is the key function of HIV gp41 during infection?
cards.back: It mediates fusion of the viral envelope with the host-cell membrane.

cards.front: Which T-cell population is preferentially infected and depleted early in mucosal HIV infection?
cards.back: Memory CD4+ T cells that express CCR5.

cards.front: What is the HIV viral set point?
cards.back: The relatively stable level of plasma viremia reached after the acute phase, which predicts the rate of disease progression.

cards.front: Why are macrophages important in persistent HIV infection?
cards.back: They support viral replication but resist the cytopathic effects that kill CD4+ T cells, so they act as reservoirs.

cards.front: What is the classic opportunistic pneumonia strongly associated with advanced AIDS?
cards.back: Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia.

cards.front: Which virus is necessary for the development of Kaposi sarcoma in AIDS?
cards.back: Human herpesvirus 8, also called Kaposi sarcoma herpesvirus.

cards.front: What stain and optical finding classically confirm amyloid?
cards.back: Congo red positivity with apple-green birefringence under polarized light.

cards.front: What is the precursor of AL amyloid?
cards.back: Immunoglobulin light chains, usually produced by a clonal plasma-cell proliferation.

cards.front: What is the precursor of AA amyloid?
cards.back: Serum amyloid-associated protein produced by the liver during chronic inflammation.

cards.front: Which protein forms the amyloid of long-term hemodialysis-associated disease?
cards.back: Beta-2-microglobulin.

cards.front: What is the most common and potentially most serious organ involved in systemic amyloidosis?
cards.back: The kidney.
